title: CRM Migration Guide: How to Switch Platforms Without Losing Data
description: A step-by-step guide to migrating between CRM platforms, from data preparation and export to validation and team adoption.
body:
Migrating between CRM platforms is a high-stakes project. Your CRM contains your team's sales history, customer relationships, and pipeline data. A poorly executed migration can result in lost opportunities, broken workflows, and reduced team productivity. This guide walks through the complete CRM migration process to ensure a smooth transition.

## Phase 1: Migration Planning

Begin by documenting your current CRM setup. Inventory all objects including contacts, companies, deals, activities, custom fields, and automation rules. Identify which data is essential to migrate and what can be archived. Define your data model in the new CRM, taking the opportunity to improve on structures that were constrained by the previous platform.

Set a realistic migration timeline. Complex CRM migrations typically require four to eight weeks from planning to full adoption. Account for data cleanup, configuration, testing, and training in your timeline.

## Phase 2: Data Cleanup and Preparation

Clean data is the foundation of a successful migration. Deduplicate contact and company records. Standardize field values and formats. Archive or remove stale records, closed-lost deals, and inactive contacts. This cleanup is one of the most valuable outcomes of a migration, as it forces teams to address data quality issues that accumulated over time.

## Phase 3: System Configuration

Configure your new CRM platform before importing data. Set up pipelines, stages, custom fields, user roles, and permission schemes. Configure automation rules for lead assignment, follow-up reminders, and deal stage transitions. Establish integrations with email, calendar, communication tools, and any other systems that connect to your CRM.

## Phase 4: Data Migration and Validation

Export your cleaned data from the legacy CRM using its export tools or API. Map fields between old and new systems, accounting for differences in data models. Import data into the new CRM using its import tools or API. After import, validate data integrity thoroughly: check record counts, verify custom field values, test automation triggers, and confirm that relationship links between records are preserved.

## Phase 5: User Training and Go-Live

Provide role-specific training on the new CRM. Focus on workflows that differ significantly from the previous platform. Plan a go-live date with clear communication to all stakeholders. Consider a parallel run period where the old system remains available for reference. Monitor adoption closely in the first weeks after migration and address questions quickly.

## Phase 6: Post-Migration Optimization

After go-live, monitor data quality, feature adoption, and user satisfaction. Gather feedback through surveys or team meetings. Adjust configurations, automation rules, and training materials based on real-world usage. Plan ongoing data hygiene reviews to maintain the improved data quality achieved through the migration process.
author: PilotStack Team
